items.title: From fake Proton VPN sites to gaming mods, this Windows infostealer is everywhere
items.description: Multiple campaigns are distributing NWHStealer through diverse platforms including fake VPN downloads, hardware utilities, and gaming modifications. The infostealer collects browser data, saved passwords, and cryptocurrency wallet information. Distribution occurs via fake websites impersonating legitimate services like Proton VPN, code hosting platforms such as GitHub and GitLab, file hosting serv
items.ai_summary: NWHStealer is a Windows infostealer that collects browser data, saved passwords, and cryptocurrency wallet information, distributed through fake VPN downloads, hardware utilities, and gaming modifications. The threat actor uses diverse platforms to spread the malware, including fake websites impersonating legitimate services. To defend against this threat, users should be cautious when downloading software from untrusted sources and verify the authenticity of websites before providing sensitive information.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1555| D[Credentials Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1021| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The n8n n8mare: How threat actors are misusing AI workflow automation
items.description: Investigation reveals widespread abuse of n8n, an AI workflow automation platform, in sophisticated phishing campaigns from October 2025 through March 2026. Attackers exploit the platform's webhook functionality to deliver malware and fingerprint devices while bypassing security filters through trusted infrastructure. Email volume containing n8n webhook URLs increased by 686% between January 2025
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are exploiting n8n's webhook functionality to deliver malware and fingerprint devices in sophisticated phishing campaigns, bypassing security filters through trusted infrastructure. The abuse of n8n's AI workflow automation platform has led to a significant increase in email volume containing n8n webhook URLs. To defend against this threat, organizations should implement robust email filtering and monitoring to detect and block suspicious n8n webhook URLs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1592| C[Discovery]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Chasing an Angry Spark
items.description: In spring 2022, a highly sophisticated backdoor named AngrySpark was discovered on a single machine in the United Kingdom. The malware employed a three-stage architecture: a DLL masquerading as a Windows Task Scheduler component, a custom virtual machine interpreter running bytecode instructions, and a beacon that profiles systems while disguising C2 communications as PNG image requests. The malwa
items.ai_summary: AngrySpark is a sophisticated backdoor malware that employs a three-stage architecture to evade detection, using a DLL to masquerade as a Windows Task Scheduler component and custom virtual machine interpreter to run bytecode instructions. The malware's real-world impact includes profiling systems and disguising C2 communications as PNG image requests. The most important defender action is to monitor for suspicious scheduled tasks and network activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1053| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Boggy Serpens Threat Assessment
items.description: The Iranian threat group Boggy Serpens, linked to the Ministry of Intelligence and Security, has refined its cyberespionage tactics to focus on trusted relationship compromises and multi-wave targeting of strategic organizations. The group combines social engineering with AI-enhanced malware for long-term persistence, primarily targeting diplomatic and critical infrastructure sectors. Recent campa
items.ai_summary: Boggy Serpens, an Iranian threat group linked to the Ministry of Intelligence and Security, uses social engineering and AI-enhanced malware for long-term persistence in trusted relationship compromises. The group targets diplomatic and critical infrastructure sectors with multi-wave attacks. Defenders should prioritize monitoring trusted relationships and user execution.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1199| B[Trusted Relationship]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: A Slopoly start to AI-enhanced ransomware attacks
items.description: IBM X-Force discovered a likely AI-generated malware named 'Slopoly' used in a ransomware attack by the Hive0163 group. This marks the beginning of AI adoption among cybercrime groups, potentially transforming the threat landscape. Slopoly, while relatively unsophisticated, demonstrates how easily threat actors can use AI to develop new malware quickly. The attack involved ClickFix social engineer
items.ai_summary: The Hive0163 group used AI-generated malware 'Slopoly' in a ransomware attack, marking the beginning of AI adoption among cybercrime groups. Slopoly, while unsophisticated, demonstrates how easily threat actors can use AI to develop new malware quickly. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for AI-generated malware and TTPs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1587.001| C[Malware]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-39987 update: How attackers weaponized marimo to deploy a blockchain botnet via HuggingFace
items.description: Three days after disclosure of a critical pre-authorization remote code execution vulnerability in the marimo Python notebook platform, multiple threat actors deployed malware hosted on HuggingFace Spaces. A previously undocumented NKAbuse variant was delivered through a typosquatted HuggingFace Space, utilizing NKN blockchain for command and control. Between April 11-14, 2026, eleven unique sourc
items.ai_summary: Attackers exploited a critical pre-auth RCE vulnerability in marimo to deploy a blockchain botnet via HuggingFace Spaces. The botnet utilized NKN blockchain for command and control. Defenders should immediately update marimo and monitor for suspicious HuggingFace Spaces activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    D -->|T1583.005| E[Botnet]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: New backdoor targeting Ukrainian entities with possible links to Laundry Bear
items.description: A new campaign targeting Ukrainian entities has been identified, attributed to actors linked to Russia. The campaign uses judicial and charity-themed lures to deploy a JavaScript-based backdoor called DRILLAPP, which runs through the Edge browser. This backdoor enables various actions including file manipulation, microphone access, and webcam capture. Two variants of the campaign have been observe
items.ai_summary: A new JavaScript-based backdoor called DRILLAPP targets Ukrainian entities, attributed to Russia-linked actors. The backdoor enables file manipulation, microphone access, and webcam capture. Defenders should monitor for user execution and remote access software TTPs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1123| C[Audio Capture]:::tactic

items.title: Hydra Saiga: Covert Espionage and Infiltration of Critical Utilities
items.description: Hydra Saiga, a suspected Kazakhstani state-sponsored threat actor, has been actively targeting government, energy, and critical infrastructure in Central Asia, Europe, and the Middle East since 2021. The group is known for using Telegram Bot API for C2 communication and employing a mix of custom implants and 'Living off the Land' techniques. Their activities align closely with Kazakhstan's geopoli
items.ai_summary: Hydra Saiga, a suspected Kazakhstani state-sponsored threat actor, uses Telegram Bot API for C2 communication and custom implants, targeting critical utilities in Central Asia, Europe, and the Middle East. Their tactics align with Kazakhstan's geopolitical interests. Defenders should monitor Telegram Bot API activity and implement 'Living off the Land' mitigation strategies.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1218| B[System Binary Proxy Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1566| D[Initial Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Execution]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Beyond the breach: inside a cargo theft actor's post-compromise playbook
items.description: A cargo theft threat actor maintained access to a decoy environment for over a month, providing extensive visibility into post-compromise operations. The attacker established redundant persistence using multiple remote access tools, including four ScreenConnect instances, Pulseway RMM, and SimpleHelp RMM. A previously unknown signing-as-a-service capability was employed to evade detection by re-si
items.ai_summary: A cargo theft threat actor maintained access to a decoy environment for over a month, using multiple remote access tools and a previously unknown signing-as-a-service capability to evade detection. This prolonged access provided extensive visibility into post-compromise operations. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for suspicious remote access tool usage and unknown signing capabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1562| B[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1566| D[Initial Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Iranian Botnet Exposed via Open Directory: 15-Node Relay Network and Active C2
items.description: An Iranian threat actor's operational infrastructure was exposed through an open directory, revealing a 15-node relay network spanning Finland and Iran, an SSH-based botnet framework, and an active command and control server. The exposed bash history documented the full operation, including tunnel deployment, DDoS tooling development, and botnet creation. The actor used on-host compilation to evad
items.ai_summary: An Iranian threat actor exposed their operational infrastructure via an open directory, revealing a 15-node relay network, SSH-based botnet framework, and active C2 server. The exposed bash history documented the full operation, including tunnel deployment, DDoS tooling development, and botnet creation. Defenders should monitor for on-host compilation and protocol tunneling.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.005| B[Botnet]:::tactic
    B -->|T1552.003| C[Bash History]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.description: Remote Code Execution Vulnerability in JP1/IT Desktop Management 2 - Manager on Windows, JP1/IT Desktop Management 2 - Operations Director on Windows, Job Management Partner 1/IT Desktop Management 2 - Manager on Windows, JP1/IT Desktop Management - Manager on Windows, Job Management Partner 1/IT Desktop Management - Manager on Windows, JP1/NETM/DM Manager on Windows, JP1/NETM/DM Client on Windows
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-65115 is a remote code execution vulnerability in various JP1/IT Desktop Management software versions on Windows. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by using T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1059 (Command and Scripting Interpreter) to gain unauthorized access. Defenders should prioritize patching affected systems to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-0740: The Ninja Forms - File Uploads plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to arbitrary f...
items.description: The Ninja Forms - File Uploads plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to arbitrary file uploads due to missing file type validation in the 'NF_FU_AJAX_Controllers_Uploads::handle_upload' function in all versions up to, and including, 3.3.26. This makes it possible for unauthenticated attackers to upload arbitrary files on the affected site's server which may make remote code execution possible. Note:
items.ai_summary: The Ninja Forms - File Uploads plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to arbitrary file uploads due to missing file type validation, potentially leading to remote code execution. Unauthenticated attackers can exploit this vulnerability to upload malicious files. Defenders should immediately update the plugin to version 3.3.27 or later.

items.title: CVE-2026-20446: In sec boot, there is a possible out of bounds write due to an integer overflow....
items.description: In sec boot, there is a possible out of bounds write due to an integer overflow. This could lead to local denial of service, if an attacker has physical access to the device, with User execution privileges needed. User interaction is not needed for exploitation. Patch ID: ALPS09963054; Issue ID: MSV-3899.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-20446 is a local denial of service vulnerability in secure boot due to an integer overflow. An attacker with physical access and user execution privileges can exploit this vulnerability. Patching is the most important defender action.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1498| C[Network Denial of Service]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-20433: In Modem, there is a possible out of bounds write due to a missing bounds check....
items.description: In Modem, there is a possible out of bounds write due to a missing bounds check. This could lead to remote escalation of privilege, if a UE has connected to a rogue base station controlled by the attacker, with no additional execution privileges needed. User interaction is needed for exploitation. Patch ID: MOLY01088681; Issue ID: MSV-4460.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-20433 is a remote escalation of privilege vulnerability in Modem due to a missing bounds check, exploitable with user interaction. An attacker can use this vulnerability to steal or forge authentication certificates (T1649). To defend, ensure timely patching with Patch ID MOLY01088681.

items.title: Newly Discovered PowMix Botnet Hits Czech Workers Using Randomized C2 Traffic
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have warned of an active malicious campaign that's targeting the workforce in the Czech Republic with a previously undocumented botnet dubbed PowMix since at least December 2025. items.title: Scans for EncystPHP Webshell, (Mon, Apr 13th)
items.description: Last week, I wrote about attackers scanning for various webshells, hoping to find some that do not require authentication or others that use well-known credentials. But some attackers are paying attention and are deploying webshells with more difficult-to-guess credentials. Today, I noticed some scans for what appears to be the "EncystPHP" web shell. Fortinet wrote about this webshell back in January. It appears to be a favorite among attackers compromising vulnerable FreePBX systems.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.
